title: Top 10 Dungeon Crawls - Part Two
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dURDUSYCm68

Daniel (@dungeondive) completes his definitive dungeon crawl rankings with the top five—and spoiler: the list is exactly what true fans probably predicted. Number five is James M. Hewitt's Hellboy, which Daniel praises for demanding genuine cooperation and tactical character balance in what could otherwise be a solitaire experience. The die system's upgrade/downgrade mechanic and card-based mission structure create dramatic arcs that keep scenarios fresh. Warhammer Quest: Silver Tower claims the number four spot, celebrated as a masterclass in elegant game design and narrative immersion from the moment you open the rulebook. Dungeon Quest from 1985—the original Games Workshop version—takes third place, embracing the purity of exploration and luck in a way that makes failure memorable and victory feel earned.

The number two slot goes to Warhammer Quest itself, the original 1995 powerhouse with its sprawling town phases, character progression, and seemingly endless campaign content that still defines what a dungeon crawl can be. But topping the list is Secrets of the Lost Tomb, a glorious, chaotic mess that throws everything at you from the first turn: rockets, magic swords, magic cats, zombies, terminators, vampires, all coated in that special Choi Wook aesthetic of barely-contained, amped-to-eleven chaos that somehow works perfectly.

----

Does Daniel's final ranking align with your own dungeon crawl preferences, and what games would crack your personal top ten list?
